title: Chapter 25: The Opening
content: The Coast opened its doors on a Saturday morning in September.
There was no ribbon-cutting. No press conference. No mayor with a giant pair of scissors.
Chloe just unlocked the front door at 8:00 AM and let the first person in.

She was a woman in her late twenties. She had heard about The Coast from a support group. She had been turned away by three other facilities because her insurance didn't cover "long-term residential mental health care."
"How much does it cost?" she asked.
"Nothing," Chloe said. "If you need help, you get help. That's the only rule."
The woman stared at her. Then she started to cry.
By noon, twelve people had walked through the doors.
By 3:00 PM, it was twenty-three.
Mia was in the server room, setting up the intake system. Amara was in the new group therapy room, leading an orientation session. Tiffany was in the kitchen, making sure the first meal was ready.
Chloe stood in the lobby and watched the building fill with people.
The building that had once been a prison was now a sanctuary.
The building that had been designed to silence women was now a place where they could speak.
The building that had tried to break her was now saving people.
She looked at the wall where she had first taped the Manifesto. The spot was empty now — the tape had been removed during demolition. But she knew what it had said.
*Rule 1: You are not crazy. You are inconvenient. Remember the difference.*
A woman walked up to her. She looked nervous. "Are you Chloe?"
"Yes."
"I read your story. Online. I was in a place like this, two years ago. A different one. But the same thing."
Chloe nodded. "I'm sorry."
"Don't be. I got out. And now I'm here. Because of you."
The woman held out her hand.
Chloe took it.
"Welcome to The Coast," she said.
At 6:00 PM, Chloe locked the front door.

Twenty-three people had come through that day. Twelve had been admitted. The others had been referred to partner organizations — The Coast couldn't help everyone, but it could make sure everyone found someone who could.
Mia came down from the server room. "Intake system is live. Medical records are encrypted. Staff scheduling is automated."
"You built all of that today?"
"I built most of it last night. Today was just testing."
Amara came out of the group therapy room. "First session went well. Seven people showed up. Two of them cried. One of them laughed. I think that's a good ratio."
Tiffany came out of the kitchen. "Dinner is ready. We have enough for everyone."
Chloe looked at them. The women who had been locked in this building with her. The women who had helped her tear it down and rebuild it.
"We did this," she said.
"We did this," they said together.
That night, after the last resident had gone to bed, Chloe sat alone in the common room.
The lights were dim. The chairs were still arranged in the circle from the orientation session. A half-empty coffee cup sat on the table.
She looked at the framed Manifesto on the wall.
*Rule 1: You are not crazy. You are inconvenient. Remember the difference.*
She had written that in a room down the hall, six weeks ago, on a piece of scrap paper, while the facility's staff thought she was sleeping.
Now it was framed. On a wall. In a building she owned.
She heard footsteps. Mia walked in, carrying two mugs of tea.
"Couldn't sleep?"
"Too much adrenaline."
Mia handed her a mug. "Same."
They sat in the circle, across from each other.
"The first day went well," Mia said.
"The first day went perfectly."
"What's tomorrow?"
Chloe sipped the tea. It was warm. Sweet. Real.
"The same thing. Just more of it."
"That sounds like a plan."
"It's not a plan. It's a routine. Plans fail. Routines last."
Mia smiled. "You sound like a CEO."
"I sound like someone who learned that the hard way."
They drank their tea in comfortable silence.
Outside, the city hummed. Inside, the building was warm and quiet.
Chloe set her mug down.
"Tomorrow, we do it again," she said.
"And the day after that."
"And the day after that."
"That's a routine."
Chloe smiled. "That's the plan."
